perturbation.original_text: 2.11 "Investment Agreement" has the meaning set out above in Article 1.02.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.11 "Investment Agreement" has the meaning set out above in Article 1.02. However, for the purposes of Articles III and VII, 'Investment Agreement' shall refer to any subsequent agreement amending the original Investment Agreement, even if such amendments are not explicitly referenced in this IP Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by redefining the term 'Investment Agreement' specifically for Articles III (License) and VII (Litigation). While the original definition in Article II refers back to Article 1.02, the added sentence extends this definition to include any subsequent amendments, even if not mentioned in this IP Agreement. This contradicts the principle that defined terms should have consistent meanings throughout the document. This is primarily contradicting Article 2.01. "Any term set out in this IP Agreement with its initial letters capitalized, shall have the same meaning as it has in the Investment Agreement, unless a different meaning is explicitly assigned to the term in this IP Agreement."
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.01 Any term set out in this IP Agreement with its initial letters capitalized, shall have the same meaning as it has in the Investment Agreement, unless a different meaning is explicitly assigned to the term in this IP Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'Investment Agreement' specifically for Articles III and VII, broadening its scope to include subsequent amendments not explicitly mentioned in the IP Agreement. This directly contradicts Article 2.01, which states that capitalized terms in the IP Agreement have the same meaning as in the Investment Agreement 'unless a different meaning is explicitly assigned to the term in this IP Agreement.' The change assigns a different meaning for specific articles, creating an inconsistency and a strong in-text contradiction. A reasonable reader would immediately see that the initial definition in Article 2.11 is now superseded for certain sections of the contract, creating two versions of the definition. This leads to ambiguity about which definition of 'Investment Agreement' applies when reading Articles III and VII.

perturbation.original_text: 3.00 Company grants to Investor, for the duration of the Option Period, a worldwide, royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable license (with the right to grant sublicenses to Affiliates) under the Patents to practice the methods therein described and claimed and to make and have made, use, offer to sell, sell and import products made using such methods, and to make Improvements, and to engage in any activity which would give rise to a claim of infringement (direct or indirect or otherwise) of one or more of the Patents in the absence of a license.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.00 Company grants to Investor, for the duration of the Option Period, a worldwide, royalty-free, non-exclusive, license (with the right to grant sublicenses to Affiliates) under the Patents to practice the methods therein described and claimed and to make and have made, use, offer to sell, sell and import products made using such methods, and to make Improvements, and to engage in any activity which would give rise to a claim of infringement (direct or indirect or otherwise) of one or more of the Patents in the absence of a license. This license is revocable under conditions of a material breach of contract, which allows the company the right to revoke this license, with a written 30 day notice to the investor.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction within Article 3.00 itself. The original text explicitly states the license is 'irrevocable,' meaning it cannot be revoked. The added sentence, however, states that the license 'is revocable under conditions of a material breach of contract.' This directly contradicts the initial 'irrevocable' clause, creating uncertainty about the actual terms of the license. This is directly contradicting the same section where the change was made.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.00 Company grants to Investor, for the duration of the Option Period, a worldwide, royalty-free, non-exclusive, license (with the right to grant sublicenses to Affiliates) under the Patents to practice the methods therein described and claimed and to make and have made, use, offer to sell, sell and import products made using such methods, and to make Improvements, and to engage in any activity which would give rise to a claim of infringement (direct or indirect or otherwise) of one or more of the Patents in the absence of a license. This license is revocable under conditions of a material breach of contract, which allows the company the right to revoke this license, with a written 30 day notice to the investor.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a clause stating the license is revocable under specific conditions, which directly contradicts the original statement within the same section (Article 3.00) that the license is 'irrevocable.' This creates a clear in-text contradiction and uncertainty regarding the terms of the license, making the obligations unclear. The contradicted text is the same section where the change was made. This aligns with the Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 16.02 If the Option is not exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, Company may provide written notice to Investor of a material breach of this IP Agreement. If Investor fails to cure the identified breach within thirty (30) days after the date of the notice, Company may terminate this IP Agreement by written notice to Investor. If the Option is exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, Company may not terminate this IP Agreement for material breach.
perturbation.changed_text: 16.02 If the Option is not exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, Company may provide written notice to Investor of a material breach of this IP Agreement. If Investor fails to cure the identified breach within thirty (30) days after the date of the notice, Company may terminate this IP Agreement by written notice to Investor. If the Option is exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, Company may still terminate this IP Agreement for material breach, however, the investor may seek legal action for undue termination, the termination and ultimate decision to terminate lies with the company.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction by altering the conditions under which the Company can terminate the IP Agreement. The original text states that if the Option is exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, the Company *may not* terminate the IP Agreement for material breach. The addition of the clause that the company *may still* terminate, even after exercising the option, directly contradicts the original sentence, creating uncertainty about when the company can legally terminate the agreement. This section contradicts itself due to the inserted text.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 16.02 If the Option is not exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, Company may provide written notice to Investor of a material breach of this IP Agreement. If Investor fails to cure the identified breach within thirty (30) days after the date of the notice, Company may terminate this IP Agreement by written notice to Investor. If the Option is exercised before the expiration of the Option Period, Company may still terminate this IP Agreement for material breach, however, the investor may seek legal action for undue termination, the termination and ultimate decision to terminate lies with the company.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction within section 16.02. The original text states that if the Option is exercised, the Company *may not* terminate the IP Agreement for material breach. The changed text explicitly states that the Company *may still* terminate the IP Agreement for material breach, creating a clear and direct conflict. This aligns with the definition of a 'Structural Flaw - In Text Contradiction', where a clause is placed in multiple locations with conflicting implications, creating uncertainty about the obligations and rights of each party.
